text.title: RACI Matrix Rules and Validation
text.summary: The four RACI roles, validation rules that must pass for every row, and the most common failure modes.

list.item: Responsible (R): does the work. Multiple R per row is allowed; this is who executes the task.
Accountable (A): final decision-maker for the task. Exactly ONE per row — this is the rule that matters most.
Consulted (C): provides input before the decision is made. Two-way communication. Minimize to essential only.
Informed (I): notified after the decision is made. One-way communication. Be generous with I.

rule.statement: Every task row must have exactly one Accountable; reject any RACI with zero or multiple A values in a single row.
rule.rationale: Zero A means no decision-maker exists; multiple A creates accountability confusion and slows decisions. The PMI PMBOK 7th edition Team Performance Domain cites this as the primary RACI failure mode.

rule.statement: Every task row must have at least one Responsible; a task with no R has no executor and will not get done.
rule.rationale: Without an R, the task is aspirationally on the matrix but not concretely assigned. It will be deprioritized or forgotten.

rule.statement: Limit Consulted roles to three per row; more than three C values creates an approval bottleneck that slows every task transition.
rule.rationale: Every C requires soliciting and integrating feedback before proceeding. Each additional C adds a round-trip delay and a potential veto point.

rule.statement: Name roles, not people, in the RACI matrix; map role-to-person in a separate table.
rule.rationale: People rotate; roles persist. A person-named matrix requires updating every time someone changes team or leaves, making it unmaintainable.

antipattern.description: A role that is "A" on more than 60% of the tasks in the matrix.
antipattern.reason: Heavy A concentration is a bottleneck indicator — that role becomes the single point of approval for most decisions. Distribute Accountable roles across appropriate decision-makers.

antipattern.description: All roles assigned "I" for a task (all-I row).
antipattern.reason: An all-I row means everyone is notified but no one executes or decides. This signals the underlying task is too vague to assign ownership.

rule.statement: Tag RACI changes in version control with the commit that introduced them; treat the RACI as code.
rule.rationale: Without version history, disputes about "who agreed to this" have no authoritative record. Git history provides the audit trail.

rule.statement: Pair RACI with an escalation path specifying who holds A when the named accountable is unavailable for more than 24 hours.
rule.rationale: Single-point accountability without an escalation path creates decision paralysis during PTO or unexpected absence.

text.reference: Lint a Markdown RACI table; exit 1 on validation failures
Blank RACI matrix template with role columns
